learning_outcomes.description: Analyse the central ideas and assumptions underlying major jurisprudential traditions, including their historical development and contemporary relevance.
Critically evaluate and compare competing theoretical perspectives on law, justice, morality, and authority, using advanced critical reasoning and intellectual independence.
Communicate jurisprudential arguments clearly, coherently, and persuasively in formats appropriate for academic or professional contexts, demonstrating ethical engagement with diverse perspectives.
Reflect on the normative, ethical, and socio-cultural implications of different theories of law, including their significance for legal practice, justice, and Australian law.

assessments.task_description:
The in-semester exam will be held during semester. This is a closed book exam. Details will be posted on Blackboard.

The examination is designed to test the students’ knowledge and understanding of the key ideas, concepts, issues and debates in legal theory and their ramifications for the practical operation of the law.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
